title: China Overtakes US in Global Favorability for First Time, Meloni Loses Electoral Reform Vote, France Legalises Assisted Dying
subhead: A Pew Research survey finds more people in 25 of 36 countries view China favourably over the US — the first such result in two decades of tracking. Italy's governing coalition suffers a one-vote defeat on electoral reform. France's National Assembly passes an assisted-dying law after years of deadlock.

sections.heading: China Beats the US in Global Favorability — A First
sections.body:
Pew Research Center's latest global attitudes survey, covering 36 countries and more than 42,000 respondents polled between February and May, has recorded a result it has never seen before: in 25 of those 36 countries, more people hold a favourable view of China than of the United States. It is the first time in Pew's tracking — which dates back to 2002 — that China has outweighed the US across so many nations at once.

Spain, Indonesia, Italy, Greece, and Canada registered the biggest swings toward China. Only six countries still favour the US more: Poland, the Philippines, South Korea, India, Japan, and Israel — all traditional security allies. Respondents in middle-income countries tended to rate China positively, while wealthier nations were more negative, with Singapore a notable exception.

Confidence in both Xi Jinping and Donald Trump was low, but Xi scored higher than Trump in most countries surveyed. A median of 75% of respondents in middle-income countries said the US interferes a great deal or a fair amount in other nations' affairs, versus 45% for China. Carnegie China scholar Chong Ja Ian noted that US policy volatility — including the use of force and resulting economic harm — has pushed many countries toward viewing Beijing as more predictable, even if not genuinely popular.
sections.whyItMatters: The survey captures a measurable shift in global public opinion at a moment when US military engagements and policy unpredictability are eroding its soft-power edge — and China is investing heavily in diplomatic outreach to fill the gap.

sections.heading: Meloni Loses Electoral Reform Vote by One Vote
sections.body:
Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's coalition government lost a secret-ballot vote on an electoral reform amendment in the Chamber of Deputies, 188 to 187 — indicating that several of her own MPs voted against the measure. The proposed reform would have moved Italy to a proportional system with a bonus for the largest party or coalition and required coalitions to agree on a common platform and single prime ministerial candidate.

Meloni called the result 'a missed opportunity for Italians' and accused opposition parties of celebrating preventing citizens from choosing their parliamentarians. Opposition leaders demanded she resign and call early elections. She did not address those calls. The defeat follows a spring referendum loss on constitutional reform — the first major blow to her government since taking power in 2022.

Coalition tensions have been mounting as Fidesz-era partners' popularity has declined. The fledgling National Future party, led by former paratrooper Roberto Vannacci, now polls at around 6%, ahead of Matteo Salvini's League at 5.6%. If elections proceed as scheduled in September 2027, Meloni would become the first Italian PM to govern a full term since 1946.
sections.whyItMatters: A one-vote defeat in a secret ballot reveals cracks inside Meloni's own coalition and suggests her government's reform agenda has hit its limits heading toward next year's election.

sections.heading: France Passes Assisted-Dying Law After Years of Deadlock
sections.body:
France's National Assembly voted 291 to 241 to approve an assisted-dying bill, clearing a legislative hurdle that had stalled for years after three rejections by the Senate. The bill allows terminally ill adults with a 'serious and incurable' illness in an advanced or terminal stage, experiencing constant and unbearable suffering resistant to treatment, to request medically assisted death.

Patients must freely express their intention to a doctor, who then has 15 days to consult before deciding. After a two-day reflection period, the patient must self-administer the lethal substance, or a physician or nurse may do so if the patient is unable. Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu announced he would refer parts of the bill to the Constitutional Council, specifically the two-day reflection window, provisions for patients under legal protection, and the role of palliative care facilities in providing assisted dying.

France now joins the Netherlands, Belgium, and several other European countries that have legalised assisted dying in some form. The Senate, dominated by right-wing parties opposed to the bill, had blocked it three times. Opinion polls show a large majority of French citizens support the change, but the Catholic Church and parts of the medical profession remain opposed.
sections.whyItMatters: After years of Senate opposition, the Assembly vote breaks a legislative logjam — but the Constitutional Council referral means the final shape of the law is still uncertain.

sections.heading: Russia Kills 14 Across Ukraine as Kyiv Strikes Black Sea Tankers
sections.body:
Russian drone and missile attacks killed at least 14 people across Ukraine overnight and into Wednesday, regional officials reported. Three were killed in Odesa when a missile struck a residential building — the fifth consecutive day Russia has hit the region. Three more died in Sumy and three in Zaporizhzhia, with further casualties in three south-eastern regions. Russia's defence ministry said it had targeted port infrastructure used for unloading petroleum products.

Ukraine's military said its drones hit 20 Russian vessels in the Black Sea overnight, including 17 oil tankers. The strikes have forced Russia to restrict shipping in the Sea of Azov, which handles roughly a quarter of its grain exports, according to Reuters. The UN reported 293 civilians killed and 1,990 injured in Ukraine in June, with long-range weapons accounting for 45% of deaths — most of them far from the front line.

On the political front, President Zelensky dismissed Prime Minister Yulia Svyrydenko after less than a year in post; parliament accepted her resignation on Tuesday. Naftogaz head Serhiy Koretskyi is the likely successor. Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov — widely credited with modernising Ukraine's armed forces — posted what appeared to be a resignation statement on Telegram. EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen arrived in Kyiv on Wednesday for talks on defence industry cooperation and Ukraine's EU accession path.
sections.whyItMatters: Dual escalation on both sides — Russia hitting civilian port cities and Ukraine striking Russian energy shipping — raises the stakes even as Ukraine undergoes a significant wartime government reshuffle.

sections.heading: Hong Kong Police Raid Independent Bookshops, Arrest Five
sections.body:
Hong Kong police raided two independent bookshops and arrested five people suspected of selling and displaying publications deemed 'seditious' under national security legislation. Authorities said the books incited 'hatred' against the government, judiciary, and law enforcement. Two men aged 37 and 57 and three women aged 30 to 59 are being held for investigation and face up to seven years in prison if convicted.

AFP reporters saw officers leading a handcuffed woman from the Have A Nice Stay bookshop in Mong Kok, which was founded in 2022 by former journalists and stocks literature on democracy and authoritarianism. The nearby Greenfield Book Store was also targeted. The Have A Nice Stay raid came one day after the shop announced it would shut down in August, citing financial reasons and an 'elusive red line' over what material is considered problematic.

This is the third such raid on Hong Kong bookshops in 2026, following arrests at Hunter in June and Book Punch in March. Amnesty International said the raids showed 'the chilling reality of what the city has become: a place where you can be criminalised simply for what is on your bookshelf.' Neither raided shop is attending this year's Hong Kong Book Fair, which opened on Wednesday.
sections.whyItMatters: The third bookshop raid in six months confirms an accelerating pattern of suppression targeting Hong Kong's remaining spaces for independent expression, deepening the chill on civil liberties.

sections.heading: US CPI Drops to 3.5%, Oil Prices Surge on Renewed Gulf Conflict
sections.body:
US consumer price inflation fell to 3.5% in the year to June, down from 4.2% in May — a bigger decline than analysts expected. The Bureau of Labor Statistics said gasoline prices dropped 9.7% in June, driven by the Iran ceasefire and memorandum of understanding that briefly lowered oil markets. Core inflation, stripping out food and energy, held steady at 2.6%.

But the reprieve may be short-lived. Brent crude surged $10 in 24 hours to hit $87 a barrel after the US launched fresh strikes on Iran and reimposed its blockade of Iranian ports. BMO Capital Markets chief US economist Scott Anderson warned that 'with fighting back on in the Gulf, the MOU in tatters, and energy prices heading higher again in July, the balance of risks remains more heavily weighted toward a rate hike at some point this year.'

New Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh told Congress the central bank has 'no tolerance to persistently elevated inflation' and stressed his commitment to independence. Fed governor Christopher Waller separately warned that policymakers may need to consider raising rates if core inflation stays elevated. More than a fifth of US small business owners reported inflation as their single most important problem — the highest share in almost two years, according to the National Federation of Independent Business.
sections.whyItMatters: June's inflation drop was real but likely already reversed by the oil price spike from renewed Iran-US hostilities — putting the Fed in a tightening bind just as the White House pressures for rate cuts.

bottomLine: The Pew survey confirming China has overtaken the US in global favourability for the first time is the day's most significant signal — not because China is suddenly admired, but because US policy volatility and military entanglements have made Beijing look comparatively predictable. In Europe, Meloni's one-vote defeat and France's assisted-dying breakthrough both reveal legislatures pushing back against executive agendas from opposite directions. Ukraine's government reshuffle and its Black Sea tanker strikes mark a pivotal week for Kyiv's wartime leadership. And the inflation data that should have been good news for the Fed is already being swallowed by oil markets reacting to the renewed Gulf conflict.
